name: Outdoor Walk
instructions: Walk outdoors at a comfortable, conversational pace on a flat, even route.
Stand tall, look ahead, relax your shoulders, and let your arms swing naturally.
Land heel to toe and breathe steadily; you should be able to talk in full sentences.
modifications: Start with 10 minutes and add a few minutes each session.
Choose a flat route before adding hills.
cautions: Wear supportive shoes and dress for the weather.
Build duration gradually.
Stop if you feel chest pain, dizziness, or unusual shortness of breath.
If you are new to exercise or have a heart/joint condition, consult a clinician first.
source: General walking-for-health guidance (public physical-activity references, e.g. CDC/Mayo Clinic walking advice). Technique described in own words.

name: Brisk Walk
instructions: Walk outdoors at a brisk pace where your breathing is noticeably faster but you can still speak in short phrases.
Pump your arms with bent elbows, keep your core lightly engaged, and take quicker, purposeful steps.
Maintain upright posture and a steady rhythm.
modifications: Alternate brisk and easy walking intervals if a continuous brisk pace is too hard.
Reduce pace anytime you can no longer talk.
cautions: Warm up with 3-5 minutes of easy walking first.
Build pace and duration gradually.
Stop if you feel chest pain, dizziness, or unusual shortness of breath.
Consult a clinician first if you are new to exercise.
source: General walking-for-health guidance (public physical-activity references). 'Brisk' = moderate-intensity 'talk test' pace. Technique in own words.

name: Treadmill Walk
instructions: Start the treadmill slowly and step on once the belt is moving.
Walk at a comfortable pace with an upright posture; let your arms swing and avoid leaning on the handrails.
Keep your gaze forward and stay centered on the belt.
modifications: Use a 0% incline to start; add a slight incline only once walking feels easy.
Hold the rail briefly to find your balance, then let go.
cautions: Clip on the safety key/lanyard so the belt stops if you slip.
Warm up at a slow speed before increasing pace.
Do not look down at your feet for long; it can throw off balance.
Stop if you feel chest pain, dizziness, or shortness of breath.
source: General treadmill form and safety guidance (public coaching/manufacturer safety references). Technique in own words.

name: Treadmill Jog
instructions: After a walking warm-up, increase the belt speed to a light jog you can sustain.
Stay tall with a slight forward lean from the ankles, land lightly under your hips, and keep a quick, relaxed stride.
Breathe rhythmically; if you cannot speak a few words, slow down.
modifications: Use walk/jog intervals (e.g. jog 1 min, walk 2 min) and build the jogging portions over time.
Lower the speed any time form breaks down.
cautions: Clip on the safety key/lanyard.
Always warm up walking before jogging and cool down after.
Do not grab the rails while jogging; reduce speed instead.
Higher impact than walking - build gradually and stop if you feel pain, chest tightness, or dizziness.
Consult a clinician before starting jogging if you have joint or heart concerns.
source: General treadmill running/jogging form and safety guidance (public coaching references). Technique in own words.

name: Stationary Bike (Steady Ride)
instructions: Set the seat so your knee is only slightly bent at the bottom of the pedal stroke.
Hold the handlebars lightly, keep your core engaged, and pedal at a smooth, steady cadence against light to moderate resistance.
Keep your knees tracking forward over your feet and avoid rocking your hips.
modifications: Lower the resistance to keep a comfortable conversational effort.
Start with shorter rides and add time gradually.
cautions: Adjust seat height before riding to protect your knees.
Warm up at low resistance for a few minutes.
Stop if you feel knee pain, chest pain, or dizziness.
Consult a clinician first if you are new to exercise.
source: General stationary-cycling setup and form guidance (public coaching references). Technique in own words.

name: E-Bike Ride
instructions: Ride your e-bike outdoors using a low pedal-assist level so you still contribute steady effort.
Keep pedaling rather than coasting to make it a workout; maintain a smooth cadence.
Stay relaxed, scan ahead for traffic and terrain, and brake early and gently.
modifications: Lower the assist level to increase effort, or raise it on hills to keep going.
Choose quiet, flat routes while you build confidence.
cautions: Wear a helmet and follow local road rules.
E-bikes are heavier and faster than they feel - brake earlier than expected.
Keep pedaling effort moderate; do not rely fully on the motor if cardio is the goal.
Stop if you feel chest pain, dizziness, or unusual fatigue.
source: General e-bike riding and safety guidance (public cycling-safety references). Technique in own words.

name: Rowing Machine (Light, Steady)
instructions: Set the resistance/damper to a LIGHT setting and strap your feet in securely.
Begin the drive with your legs by pushing through your heels, then lean back slightly from the hips, and finish by pulling the handle to your lower ribs.
Reverse the order to recover: extend your arms, hinge forward from the hips, then bend your knees to slide back up.
Keep your back neutral (not rounded) and your core braced throughout; aim for a smooth, unhurried stroke.
modifications: Slow the stroke rate and shorten the range while learning the sequence.
Start with 5-10 minutes and add time gradually.
cautions: Keep resistance LIGHT as noted by the owner.
Drive with your legs, not your back; keep your spine neutral and avoid yanking with your arms or rounding/hunching.
Do not over-grip the handle.
Warm up with a few easy strokes first.
Stop if you feel lower-back, knee, chest pain, or dizziness.
Consult a clinician first if you have back issues or are new to exercise.
source: General indoor-rowing form and safety guidance (public coaching references; leg-drive sequence and neutral-spine cues). Light resistance per owner preference. Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Row
instructions: Sit on the floor with legs extended and knees slightly bent, looping the band around both feet, or anchor the band at chest height and stand facing it.
Hold an end in each hand with arms extended, chest lifted, and core lightly braced.
Pull your elbows back toward your ribs and gently squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Return slowly with control, keeping light tension through your back.
modifications: Use a lighter band or choke up on it to reduce tension.
Beginners start with 2-3 sets of 10-15 reps.
cautions: Warm up your shoulders first.
Keep your spine neutral and avoid shrugging or jerking.
Inspect the band for nicks before use so it cannot snap.
Stop if you feel joint pain.
source: General resistance-band rowing form guidance (Healthline 'Seated Resistance Band Row', SET FOR SET band-row guide). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Chest Press
instructions: Anchor the band behind you (around your upper back while lying down, or at a door anchor at chest height while standing/split-stance).
Hold an end in each hand at chest level with elbows bent.
Press your hands forward until your arms are nearly straight, squeezing your chest.
Return slowly with control to the start.
modifications: Use a lighter band or step closer to the anchor to reduce tension.
Perform standing in a staggered stance for more stability.
cautions: Warm up your shoulders first.
Keep wrists straight and shoulders down; do not let the band pull your arms back too fast.
Inspect the band and anchor before use.
Stop if you feel shoulder pain.
source: General resistance-band chest-press form guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Overhead Press
instructions: Stand on the middle of the band with feet shoulder-width apart, holding an end in each hand at shoulder height.
Brace your core and keep your ribs down so you do not arch your lower back.
Press your hands straight overhead until your arms are nearly extended.
Lower slowly back to shoulder height with control.
modifications: Use a lighter band or a narrower stance to reduce tension.
Press one arm at a time to start.
cautions: Warm up your shoulders first.
Avoid arching your lower back - keep your core tight.
Keep the band secured under both feet so it cannot slip out.
Stop if you feel shoulder or neck pain.
source: General resistance-band overhead-press form guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Pull-Apart
instructions: Stand tall holding the band with both hands at shoulder height, arms extended in front of you, palms down.
Keeping your arms straight, pull the band apart by moving your hands out to your sides and squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Return slowly with control, keeping light tension on the band.
modifications: Widen your grip on the band to reduce tension.
Beginners start with 2-3 sets of 12-15 reps.
cautions: Warm up your shoulders first.
Keep your shoulders down and avoid shrugging toward your ears.
Move smoothly; do not let the band snap back.
Stop if you feel shoulder pain.
source: General resistance-band pull-apart form guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Squat
instructions: Place a loop band just above your knees (or stand on a long band holding the ends at your shoulders) with feet shoulder-width apart.
Lower into a squat by bending your knees and pushing your hips back, keeping your chest up.
Press your knees slightly outward against the band so they do not cave in.
Push through your heels to return to standing.
modifications: Use a lighter loop band or omit the band to start.
Squat to a higher depth if your knees or hips are uncomfortable.
cautions: Warm up first.
Keep your knees tracking over your toes and your back neutral.
Do not let the loop band roll up or down off your thighs.
Stop if you feel knee or back pain.
source: General resistance-band squat form guidance (gethealthyu / Sweat band-squat references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Glute Bridge
instructions: Lie on your back with knees bent and feet flat, placing a loop band just above your knees.
Press your knees gently outward against the band to keep tension.
Squeeze your glutes and push through your heels to raise your hips into a straight line from knees to shoulders.
Lower slowly with control.
modifications: Omit the band to start, or use a lighter loop.
Hold the top position briefly for extra glute work.
cautions: Keep the lift driven by your glutes, not your lower back.
Do not over-arch at the top.
Stop if you feel lower-back discomfort.
source: General resistance-band glute-bridge form guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Lateral (Monster) Walk
instructions: Place a loop band just above your knees or around your ankles and stand with feet hip-width apart in a quarter-squat.
Keep tension on the band and step one foot out to the side, then bring the other foot in, maintaining the band stretch.
Take several steps in one direction, then return in the other direction, staying low with your chest up.
modifications: Place the band above the knees (easier) rather than at the ankles (harder).
Use a lighter loop band.
cautions: Warm up first.
Keep your knees pushed slightly outward and toes forward; do not let knees cave in.
Stay in a controlled quarter-squat rather than bobbing up and down.
Stop if you feel knee or hip pain.
source: General resistance-band lateral/monster-walk form guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Biceps Curl
instructions: Stand on the middle of the band with feet hip-width apart, holding an end in each hand with arms down and palms facing forward.
Keeping your elbows pinned to your sides, curl your hands up toward your shoulders.
Lower slowly with control back to the start, keeping light tension.
modifications: Use a narrower foot stance or lighter band to reduce tension.
Curl one arm at a time.
cautions: Keep your elbows still and avoid swinging your torso.
Control the lowering phase; do not let the band snap your arms down.
Stop if you feel elbow or wrist pain.
source: General resistance-band biceps-curl form guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Triceps Extension
instructions: Anchor the band overhead (or hold one end behind your head with the other hand) so tension pulls your forearm down.
Keep your upper arm still and your elbow pointing up or forward.
Extend your forearm until your arm is straight, squeezing the triceps.
Bend slowly back to the start with control.
modifications: Use a lighter band or move closer to the anchor.
Perform one arm at a time.
cautions: Keep your upper arm and elbow stationary; only the forearm moves.
Avoid arching your lower back on overhead variations.
Stop if you feel elbow pain.
source: General resistance-band triceps-extension form guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Romanian Deadlift
instructions: Stand on the middle of the band with feet hip-width apart, holding an end in each hand at the front of your thighs, knees softly bent.
Hinge at your hips by pushing your hips backward (not squatting down), letting your hands slide down the front of your legs.
Keep your back flat and lower until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings.
Drive your hips forward to return to standing, squeezing your glutes.
modifications: Use a lighter band and a shorter range to learn the hip hinge.
Place a chair behind you to practice the backward hip motion.
cautions: The hips move BACKWARD (hinge), not down like a squat.
Keep your spine neutral and flat - do not round your back.
Build range gradually.
Stop if you feel lower-back pain.
Consult a clinician first if you have back issues.
source: General band Romanian-deadlift form guidance (The Complete Athlete Guide 'How to Do an RDL' hip-hinge cues). Technique in own words.

name: Resistance Band Lat Pulldown (Door-Anchored)
instructions: Anchor the band at the top of a closed door (using a door anchor) and kneel or sit facing the door.
Hold an end in each hand with arms extended overhead and a slight forward lean.
Pull your elbows down and toward your sides, drawing your hands toward your shoulders and squeezing your lats.
Return slowly with control to the overhead start.
modifications: Move farther from the door or use a lighter band to reduce tension.
Pull to chest height rather than full range while learning.
cautions: Use a proper door anchor and make sure the door is fully closed and latched so the band cannot release.
Keep your shoulders down and core braced; avoid leaning back excessively.
Stop if you feel shoulder pain.
source: General band lat-pulldown / door-anchor form and safety guidance (public beginner band-workout references). Technique in own words.
